speaker: interviewee
text: I understand it's really not related. You only have to go in a few more years, a few more years. We don't have to go in today very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unless you want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six, interview with Henry--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five. Two, three, four, five.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five, interview with Henry Moser, and it's January 24th, 1996. And Henry, can you tell me about the end of the war in Shanghai? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the end of the war came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of unexpected when we heard about the atomic bomb. We heard about it, and the word atomic bomb was mentioned, and we-- we asked the engineer for-- for my boss. We-- we-- we asked him, is there such thing? He said-- he thought it was just a very powerful bomb. He didn't think it was atomic. That was-- he just didn't know. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we thought it was over, there were certain-- it wasn't. The police was gone for a while, and there was some kind of a riot, some-- some Jewish people whose friends or family were killed by the Japanese. Particularly, there was-- there were a couple of Russian policeman still at the police who turned them in because they came late, or when they had a passport to leave, they came late over the border. I did not mention-- unfortunately, there was really no time-- the-- the-- the Japanese police and Mr. Ghoya, who called himself the King of the Jews and who gave those permissions.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is a chapter in itself again, which-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those people any-- anyway, it was a lot of chicanery there. And they wanted to get even, particularly wanted to get those two Russian policeman. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was after the first time, after the first bomb, which was-- was it Nagasaki or Iwo-- Iwo Jima? I'm not sure which one was the first one. We know there were two.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it turned out, luckily, nothing happened There was a-- there was a-- a-- a-- a-- one man stopped this kind of lynching mob, luckily. The Chinese police were in back. They won't get involved with foreigners. And they knew darn well that those two Russian had done evil things. But it was all over after-- after they came, a man that was pretty well liked, a big man, and said, you better go home. Nothing has changed. And sure enough, the Japanese police was back within a few hours. And we didn't know anything till-- what was over, just rumors, till the second atomic bomb was dropped. Then the news, as-- as you may know, the-- the-- the Emperor of Japan ended the war after the second bomb. So it-- it took a while between the first and second bomb. After that, we-- for another six weeks, we-- we got some information. There were some leaflets dropped, I believe from American planes, to-- to give us hope and so on, basically. But-- but even the news media, what was available there, told us too. There were still-- the last few weeks, the Russians were involved in the war. But there was-- there were, by that time, the news media was strictly-- what we heard was it was either Japanese or German. But they didn't keep it too much of a secret. And I-- the details I forgot. The fact was, there was a deal made with the Japanese for the next six weeks to keep it-- to stay in the city and keep law and order, which they did. Meanwhile, the Japanese-- mean-- meanwhile, everybody had a ball in Shanghai because the Japanese did open their warehouses to-- to the Chinese employees. Didn't pay them money or in anything else and told them, help yourself. And all that stuff-- canned food, uniforms, clothing, all kinds, men, even women clothing-- everything was being sold on the street for next to nothing. Everybody had a ball. Japanese didn't care. And until the Chinese troops came in, American troops came in, and Chinese troops came in. Officially, the Chinese troops came in for face saving device for the Chiang Kai-shek. Of course, they had to let the Chinese troops in first. But American troops were there too. They did not occupy the city. They just-- they just occupied certain-- certain areas-- the bases, especially where the Japanese used to be. And they evacu-- it was also the job of the American ships to evacuate the Japanese back to Japan. Meanwhile, they had them work there tearing down the bunkers they had built on the harbors. I worked for the Army at the time at the port command-- American port command. And we built fences for-- for the Chinese 12th army at that time, to be sent north to fight the Communists. I've never heard of a battle being fought there, and I have a pretty good guess what happened to those soldiers. They turned around. They were treated very badly by their officers, which I witnessed because I was in those warehouses. We-- we put them-- we built those fences so they went on the LSDs where they shipped them, where they shipped north, with all I knew. They were equipped by American-- by the American army. And they were-- they sprayed them. They didn't want-- whatever, any-- any-- any-- any-- any lice or whatever those people may have had. They were a poor bunch of people, in my eyes. And I saw them inside, the way they were-- the way they were treated by their officers-- very badly. So whatever happened then, I don't know. That's history. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see, specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd see officers treated their-- treated their men like dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did they do? What-- what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't quite understand how they talked to them and so on. But it was-- it was obviously they were mis-- they were not treated very nicely, OK. I don't remember many details. I said, my God, they treat them. That's what I remember-- how they treat them. I was once inside a warehouse there, where the-- where the Chinese were stationed before they shipped them up there. They-- they weren't very well treated. And obviously, they were not-- not enthusiastic followers of Chiang Kai-shek anymore. And within-- Chiang Kai-shek was a big hero when the war was over when-- when-- among the Chinese in Shanghai. There was a big VE Day, I remember. And then Chiang Kai-shek started to have universal military draft. And the Chinese soldier was, up to that time, regarded as a-- as very low, right above the beggar, the professional soldier. And the Chinese didn't like that at all. What was worse is that-- that a lot of the Chinese army, they came to town to amuse themselves on weekends or on the day off. And movie theaters were limited in Shanghai, for instance. And they forced their way into the crowded movie theaters. There was a fire department there that didn't allow anybody to stand just like here. But they-- they were shooting. And later, they took their guns away. I understand they sold even hand grenades in some of the theaters. So they were very, very much unliked. And naturally, they blamed Chiang Kai-shek. Right or wrong, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Chinese, I worked later on at the Naval base [INAUDIBLE]. They had no use for Chiang Kai-shek anymore. That's the everyday Chinese which I talked to. And they-- he became, within one year, a very well-hated man. So the situation in-- in-- in Shanghai was-- was again changing. Tell me about-- you mentioned the Japanese police and a Mr. Ghoya?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Ghoya was-- was the-- was set in charge of-- of-- officially of giving or denying passports for people who wanted to leave the restricted area for purpose of work. If you were well dressed, you were treated quite usually like dirt. He would slap people around. If you were dirty dressed and did work like my boss-- he liked my boss-- and I-- he treated them well. He was-- he was-- he was probably a psychopath. He spoke English, maybe even spoke a few words German. He was not the boss there. There were two other people above him who were much more dangerous, but they stayed out. They didn't have much-- they didn't get in touch with us so much. An incident [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about Mr. Ghoya was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we saw command we knew. There was a-- we had our own security to guard our borders. We had to form what they called [NON-ENGLISH]. The Chinese had that too, but we were-- our job was to guard the borders of the restricted area that nobody left and nobody came in late for passport. When somebody came in late with a passport, we probably wouldn't have done anything as long as there was no police around, like-- like those Russian policemen. But usually, that didn't happen. And I had to stand guard, too, so many hours a week. And the-- the camp we lived in had a big gate and a small one. The small gate was at the end of the restricted area, and I stood guard there. And one-- one day, I stood guard there and Mr. Ghoya comes by on his bicycle. He did ride a bicycle all over the place. And he asked me to get out a Mr. [? Tushinsky. ?] There were two Russians that took over the Jewish sports club because the Japanese closed us down as punishment. This is now still during the war. So the Russians took-- which were Soviet citizens, evidentally. And the Japanese were no trouble to Soviet citizens.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the man didn't want to go in. He was-- evidently, he had a meeting scheduled with Mr. Tushinsky or Yanovich, I forgot which one. And I didn't know anything about it. I knew there was a little clubhouse in there. I knew the Russians had taken over the sports club and we played soccer again after they stopped us. So he-- he told me to get-- to get the men out. So I said, I can't leave here. He said, I'll stand guard for you. He did. I went outside and I called, went in this house, and asked for the name. I didn't meet him before. And the men came out and he-- he asked very friendly Mr. Ghoya to come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he came in. He was about six foot five and Mr. Ghoya was about four foot 10. It was a nice picture. Too bad I couldn't take a picture of that. That was a personal experience I had. Another personal-- I didn't have any other personal experience with him. People had very bad experiences. Some people were slapped around. Some people were thrown into a jail, and some people died in that jail because they supposedly infested the jail with lice carrying ty-- typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his position again?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a plainclothes police-- police in charge of giving or denying passports to us. That's really what he was. He also played the violin and when they invited him to-- to play the violin sometimes at some performances-- we had musician's performances-- he didn't play the violin very well. He was-- he was giving his-- but he could have stopped the performances. So they sometimes invited him. And he was so happy to play the violin, or acted so happy, that I think he was-- he had-- something was mighty wrong with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to when the war ended. And did you stay there? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there. When the war ended, we had no place to go. There were very few people that had a place to go. We were still stateless refugees. There was some openings. There was some stories about Australia would take some young people in. And I applied for immigration, and indeed a number of them got the permit later on. But I-- meanwhile, I had an affidavit from a friend of mine to go to the United States. And-- but this was almost two years later. In the first years, things didn't change very much at all except that we got [INAUDIBLE] food package, the-- the surplus army food, surplus armed forces food. And that was-- I wouldn't say there [INAUDIBLE]. There was much, because we didn't have enough. We-- almost everybody was underweight. Most children tested positive-- most small children in the camp I lived tested positive for TB. If they didn't have it, they tested-- probably the test, I don't know. The poor thing, when they got decent food, it went away. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we got well fed and a lot of us worked. The younger people worked for the armed forces and we got paid-- when I was working for the army, we got paid in American money. And we still stayed in camp. We still weren't affluent, but we-- life had changed. It was much better, but we didn't want to stay there. And so far, we saw no opening. Palestine was still basically closed, as you-- as you remember, probably, or know-- was still basically closed for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to back-- going back to Germany, who wanted to go back to Germany and Austria? There were individuals who did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So until about early '47, late '46, not many people left. Then it began-- all things began opening up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly, Harry Truman did sign into law, and I think Senator Jacob Jeffers and Congressman Emanuel Celler-- I don't know if you remember those names-- helped with that, that they would use a quota, yes. You see, there was a quota for immigrants. An affidavit without a quota, your number was no good. You had to wait. The German quota was approximately 14,000 a year. Quota was based on 1920-- the-- the geographical condition in 1920. Many Jews who were-- German Jews-- lived in-- Poland was-- did not-- that was one of the times when Poland was occupied part by Germany, part by Austria, and part by Russia. So after World War I, Poland was existing again. But the Jews who lived there remembered the pogroms. They didn't want any part of Russia, they didn't want any part of Poland, and they figured they prefer to stay in Germany. They were German speaking. There was a province of Posen in West Prussia, and in Austria, Galicia. Now, those people went to-- most of them went to Australia or Germany. And the American quota system was such that they fell under Polish quota because it was 1920 Poland. Polish quota was about 4,000, and there were many more Polish Jews under the Polish quota than the German. So this is-- this is why it was difficult. So then, when finally [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in-- when Israel was established, I was out of Shanghai. I was here. But that's when many were able to go to Israel, finally, and particularly, they had that fell under this quota law. But being under German quota was-- was-- was a break, because Truman signed into law at that time that they could use two or three more years where there were no immigrants and add them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So instead of 14,000, they were allowed 42,000 Jews for the German quota, for instance, or in Poland, instead of 4,000, 12,000. So some-- some of us could go. And many of the ones for the German quota could go. When did you find out, or how did you find out, about what happened in Germany and [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: The concentration-- well, after the war, the stories came through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody that was involved that didn't come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. Family of mine, a lot of families, a lot of my-- my mother's family-- I mean, on my mother's side, as well from my father's side. And many, many. There was a cousin of my father who did come back, who wrote the first letter how conditions were in Germany from 1946. I have that letter, but it's written in German. I published it in the paper in Shanghai, because it was the first, actually, report mainly about Germany. But we knew that she survived because she contacted us. Her husband got killed-- first husband got killed. But she survived. Her brother got killed, who was very close with us. He got killed. That she could tell that to us. Other people-- we heard it only later, some people just were not around anymore. We-- we-- we think they got killed. We know. [INAUDIBLE] My mother, uncles, children, grandchildren, grandchild in one case and so on, they all-- obviously, they got killed. Nobody ever got in touch with us. And we looked for many of them. They were just gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] parents lost their--

speaker: interviewee
text: They lo-- they-- yeah. My mother, her brothers, both survived and family. My father's brother survived. He was in Palestine. He was in concentration camp before the war. She had some brothers in there who guaranteed to-- had to put up 1,000 pounds or something so he could get out of the concentration camp and-- and he left in 19-- early 1939. He went to Palestine. So they survived, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents by that time were all dead. My grandmother died late in 1938 after the crystal night.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back then, when-- now, when did you leave Shanghai, and tell me about that. Were your parents with you? How did you get over?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- I got an affidavit to a former friend of mine. He was in the Los Angeles area. He lived in South Gate. And he was my best friend during school years. He was the one I went to temple-- temple with every weekend, you know. And we were very close friends. And he gave me-- he lived in the United States at the time. And he was a citizen. And my parents had nothing. But I knew at that time that the United Service for New Americans, established by I don't know who, gave affidavits. Because they had the quo-- now it's the reverse. People had-- before, they had affidavits and they had to wait for the quota. Now they had an open quota, almost, because there were enough numbers there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they gave affidavits to-- to people as an organization. And my parents-- I-- I wouldn't have left them if I would have known that shortly [INAUDIBLE] they were promised to get-- to get the affidavit and get the visa to the United States. That's how we wound up here.

speaker: interviewee
text: United Service for New Americans was established for getting people in that didn't have any other people guaranteeing for them. Because at that time, there were strict immigration laws, not like today. And-- and also, we came to San Francisco and they tried, because of fear of antisemitism over here, they didn't want too many people in one spot. But I think in San Francisco, maybe close to 2,000 people stayed there anyway. They couldn't force them either. They did-- they wanted to distribute them all over the whole country and found jobs for them. It was-- wasn't a-- was a good thing. But a lot of people liked it in San Francisco, found jobs there, and stayed after the war. The job situation was good for everybody. And of course I had a friend here, so my parents came to Los Angeles because I had a friend here and I have an affidavit. But they-- they-- who-- the people they brought in who had nobody else and-- and-- and didn't insist in staying in San Francisco-- we all came to San Francisco-- they got very good jobs, very off, all over the country. It-- it was a wonderful thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get here? On a ship, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a ship. Was-- was one of-- one of the one of the-- I guess you call them Liberty Ships. One of the troop transporters. Marine Adder was its name, was the name of the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you speak English when you got here, or what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, enough to get around, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened from there. You got here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Got here, got myself a job, was stu-- stuck in a trade. And I was really never sorry I did. I was really never sorry I did. Some people said [SIGHS] if you work with your hands, you'll be regarded as somebody dumb or something like this. I have-- I was already in the trade. I was in the union.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in sheet metal worker's union. And I had never any real problem. I had never any real problem. And I don't know if I should mention this, but I'd like to. There was a-- there was a 1933 club, which still exists here, for refugees from-- from-- from Germany, [INAUDIBLE] and they had a youth group. And that-- we had a speaker from the Jewish Community Council at that time. That was before they were on 590 North Vermont, even before that. And they advised people to stay away from the trades. Jews-- Jews would be mistreated and should stay out of the trades. I never forget that. I've-- too bad I forgot the name of the man-- the name of the man who said that nonsense remark. I met many Jewish people in the trade that were born and raised here. I did not have any problems. I have heard remarks, antisemitic remarks, at one time or another. And I stood up and said, I'm sorry, I'm Jewish. Oh, they apologized.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what can you do? You live in this world. The way-- people are the way they are. But when they apologized, and some-- some-- some-- some are kind of sheepishly apologized, I accept it. It didn't happen very often. Really, I must say, it did not happen very often.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't stick my nose out of business-- out of other-- I didn't stick my nose in other people's business. I mind my own business. I got along with everybody. I had no problems. And I made good money. And when I retired from the trade, I went to my own business for 11 years. I had a locksmith's business here. I went and I enjoyed it very much. But-- but I was-- was really never sorry. I could support my family. When my kids were little, my wife didn't have to work. We had a nice home. We had everything we needed. We had two cars. I-- I didn't have-- my children, when they got out of high school, they started to have-- started to work part-- part time to pay for college. They helped. I helped. They stayed still in my house and so on. And, like I said, no. Some people made more-- made more money than I, I'm sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm not sorry. And if I would live my life over again, I'm not so sure of what I would do, but I'm not sorry that I-- that I-- that I choose to stay in a trade. Why-- how I got into a trade? My whole family was-- was not in a trade. Also, my-- my-- my father's family back there, they-- they did-- what is it-- what do you call them now? They cure skins, make leather-- leather? I forgot now what you call them. Not farriers, but rather making-- lea-- leather factories, you know. They were in the leather business, evidently for a long time. Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but I-- but otherwise, later on, people went out and became-- became salespeople and-- and-- and merchants and so on. And when it came to my father, my father was so old. You don't know a trade, nobody wanted you. People who had a trade could leave Germany. That's when I learned the trade. That's when I went to the [INAUDIBLE] in Germany, and I had a chance to go to England right before the war with the [INAUDIBLE]. But-- but our boat left before. And war-- we knew war was-- was somehow imminent. So we-- so-- so I went with my parents to Shanghai. But some other kids from the [INAUDIBLE] went to England. They helped them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So your parents followed you back to L.A., and then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just-- just-- just three months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and then-- and then you lived there and you worked. When did you marry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joyce Lafazan was her name. [LAUGHS] She-- they were Sephardic Jews. They-- her parents came from Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, a son and a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ronald and Ruth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Rachel and Emily, my son's two daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you say-- do you think about the Shoah often, and what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think how lucky I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has it affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: That question isn't easy to answer. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It has affected me in such a way that I learned to live-- try to live every day, and not to plan too far in the future. Although my wife and other people will disagree there. I-- when I came to this country here, I was very happy. And when the war in Korea broke out, I was very unhappy because I thought, there's no end to it, and this Russian threat, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I told myself, what am I complaining about? I lived from one day to the next in Shanghai. And I wasn't that unhappy. From now on, that's how I live. And if you ask me, how did it affect me, I guess that's pinpointing it very well. That's how it affected me, and still does. Have you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I live from one day to the next, and I-- and I refuse to worry about the next day. Although that's-- you can't-- that doesn't mean I don't worry. You-- you refuse to. You get to that point. And this was, strangely enough, that what set me off in that direction was the Korean war in 1950. I said, there's no end to this. Well, there is no peace in this world. There is no-- you just live one day. Don't-- don't bother to plan ahead of time. That doesn't mean I wouldn't plan ahead of time. But I mean, still, live your-- try to live your day as good as you can every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and-- and this had affected me. Was I able to follow through? Probably not very much. But it still-- still affected me, my thinking. I still will say that's the only way to be. Were you involved in the Korean war at all? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not involved. I lucked out. I was going to be drafted, and I accepted that. But there was selective service at the time. This was right before the Korean War. OK, I'm sorry. It was before the Korean war with Selective Service. So, since because of my hearing in the left ear, they rejected me. So I became 4F. Later on, it was, I think, 5A or something like that. They changed it later on. But by that time, I was older. And the Korean war was-- that was in 1949, I think. And the Korean war broke out in 1950. I wasn't a standby. You know, I had to report. But they never-- they never called me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I guess I-- I was lucky, because a friend of mine got hurt. I don't know anybody who got killed there, a friend, but several friends of mine were in Korea and went through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a good time to change tape.
